schools.summary: Paul opens by asserting his apostleship — called (divinely summoned, not self-appointed) to be an apostle of Christ 'through the will of God,' his commission resting on God's sovereign appointment; joining Sosthenes (likely the former synagogue ruler, Acts 18:17) as a brother known to the Corinthians (Barnes, Robertson).
Chrysostom (with Tertullian): Paul stresses his divine calling at the outset because the faction-prone Corinthians needed reminding of his God-given authority.

themes: Paul, called to be an apostle of Jesus Christ through the will of God
Sosthenes our brother
Paul's apostolic greeting
the divine ground of his apostleship

schools.summary: Paul addresses 'the church of God' (not of Paul or any party) at Corinth — a pointed reminder against their factions — those 'sanctified in Christ Jesus' (consecrated to God in him) and 'called to be saints' (summoned to holiness), together with all who call on Christ's name everywhere (Barnes, Robertson).
Cyril (with Calvin): 'calling on the name of Jesus Christ' implies his deity, prayer being offered to him; the letter belongs to all believers everywhere, not Corinth only.

themes: the church of God which is at Corinth
them that are sanctified in Christ Jesus, called to be saints
the address to the sanctified church
calling on the name of Jesus

schools.summary: Paul's customary greeting: grace (God's free favour) and peace (the reconciliation and tranquillity that flow from it), the sum of gospel blessing, coming alike from the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ — the two named together as one source, a mark of Christ's divine dignity (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: grace precedes peace, for God's free favour is the fountain from which all peace flows; the blessing wholly of grace.

themes: Grace be unto you, and peace
from God our Father, and from the Lord Jesus Christ
the benediction of grace and peace
grace the fountain of peace

schools.summary: Paul, before any rebuke, begins with thanksgiving — constantly giving thanks to God on their behalf, a gracious opening even to a faulty church — for the grace (the spiritual gifts and favour) bestowed on them through Christ, all their endowments owing to God's grace, not their merit (Barnes, Robertson).
Chrysostom (with Calvin): Paul acknowledges what is good in them before correcting their faults, and ascribes all their gifts to grace, gently undercutting their pride.

themes: I thank my God always on your behalf
for the grace of God which is given you by Jesus Christ
Paul's thanksgiving for grace
praise before rebuke

schools.summary: The Corinthians were enriched (made abundant) by Christ in everything — particularly in 'all utterance' (the gift of speech, expression) and 'all knowledge' (understanding of divine truth); their abundance of gifts coming from Christ, not their own attainment (Barnes, Robertson).
Wesley: this church was notably rich in spiritual gifts (utterance and knowledge), though, as the epistle shows, lacking in love and order.

themes: in every thing ye are enriched by him
in all utterance, and in all knowledge
the Corinthians enriched in utterance and knowledge
the wealth of their endowment

schools.summary: 'The testimony of Christ' (the gospel witness concerning Christ which Paul preached) was confirmed (established, made firm) in them by their reception of it and the spiritual gifts that accompanied it; the gospel confirmed both by their faith and by the gifts that attested its truth and power (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Wesley): the enrichment of the Corinthians was the seal and confirmation of the gospel testimony among them — their gifts proving the genuineness of the word they received.

themes: the testimony of Christ was confirmed in you
the testimony of Christ confirmed among them
the gospel established by faith and gifts
the seal of their endowment

schools.summary: The Corinthians fell short in no spiritual gift (charisma) — richly endowed, behind no other church in the gifts of the Spirit — while 'waiting for' (eagerly expecting) the revelation (coming) of Christ, their gifts held in an attitude of expectant hope toward his return (Barnes, Robertson).
Cyril (with Calvin): the right use of present gifts is joined to the expectation of Christ's appearing; the Corinthians, for all their endowment, were to live looking for his coming.

themes: ye come behind in no gift
waiting for the coming of our Lord Jesus Christ
lacking no gift while awaiting Christ
endowment held in expectant hope

schools.summary: God (or Christ) shall confirm (establish, strengthen) the believers unto the end, keeping them firm to the close of life, that they may be found blameless (free from accusation, acquitted) in the day of Christ — the day of judgment, when they shall stand unaccused (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin: this confirming is God's work, not man's — the saints' perseverance to the end and their blamelessness at the last rest on God's faithful keeping; the Arminian reading takes it as the promise to those who continue.

themes: Who shall also confirm you unto the end
that ye may be blameless in the day of our Lord Jesus Christ
confirmed blameless to the end
God's faithful keeping

schools.summary: Paul grounds the assurance of v.8 in God's faithfulness — God is faithful (true to his promises, unchanging) and therefore will surely keep those he has called; the believers effectually called by grace into fellowship (communion, participation) with Christ (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Cyril): since God who called them is faithful, he will not abandon his own work; the calling is into union and communion with the Son, sharing in him and his benefits.

themes: God is faithful, by whom ye were called
unto the fellowship of his Son Jesus Christ our Lord
God's faithfulness the ground of security
calling into fellowship with Christ

schools.summary: Paul earnestly entreats them, by the name (authority) of Christ, to speak the same thing (be of one accord, ending their party slogans), that there be no 'divisions' (schismata, factions) tearing the church, but that they be perfectly joined together in the same mind and judgment, a complete restoration of harmony (Barnes, Robertson).
Chrysostom (with Henry): the believers are to be knit together (as a body, or a mended net) in the same disposition and opinion — Paul pleads for the healing of their rents in love.

themes: that ye all speak the same thing, and that there be no divisions among you
perfectly joined together in the same mind and in the same judgment
the plea for unity against divisions
the call to harmony

schools.summary: Paul names his informants — members of the household of Chloe (a Corinthian woman, perhaps a merchant whose people travelled) had reported the 'contentions' (erides, strifes, quarrels) among the believers; the factions having erupted into open disputes, Paul dealing not in rumour but a credible report (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: Paul openly names his source, neither concealing the trouble nor dealing in vague hearsay — a model of honest, loving rebuke.

themes: it hath been declared unto me of you... by them which are of the house of Chloe
that there are contentions among you
the report of contentions
Paul's candid dealing

schools.summary: The Corinthians had split into parties, each rallying under a name: 'I am of Paul' (the founder), 'of Apollos' (the eloquent teacher), 'of Cephas' (Peter, perhaps the Jewish-leaning), and 'of Christ' (a party claiming Christ exclusively, perhaps with spiritual pride) — even this last at fault, making Christ a party-name (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Wesley): the factions arose from attaching themselves to human teachers (and even abusing Christ's name) rather than to Christ as the one head of all.

themes: I am of Paul; and I of Apollos; and I of Cephas; and I of Christ
the party slogans dividing the church
the divisive party spirit
attaching to men rather than Christ

schools.summary: Paul exposes the folly of faction by three questions: 'Is Christ divided?' (Christ is one; to split into parties rends his body, an absurdity); 'was Paul crucified for you?' (it was Christ, not Paul, who died for them); 'were ye baptized in the name of Paul?' (they were baptized into Christ's name, pledged to him alone, Barnes, Robertson).
Cyril (with Calvin): they were baptized into Christ's name, not Paul's — belonging wholly to Christ, who alone was crucified for them; the party spirit denies this.

themes: Is Christ divided? was Paul crucified for you?
were ye baptized in the name of Paul
the three questions exposing faction
Christ alone their owning Lord

schools.summary: Paul recalls that he personally baptized very few at Corinth — only Crispus (the former synagogue ruler, Acts 18:8) and Gaius — so none could claim to be his party through his baptizing them; thankful not that baptism is unimportant, but that providentially he had baptized so few that none could pretend he sought disciples to himself (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Henry): Paul does not disparage baptism, but rejoices that the circumstance removed all ground for the faction that bore his name.

themes: I thank God that I baptized none of you, but Crispus and Gaius
Paul's thankfulness that he baptized few
no party formed through baptism
baptism not disparaged

schools.summary: Paul states why he is glad he baptized few: lest anyone should say he had baptized in his own name — that is, to make disciples to himself, gathering a following around his own person; the danger being that baptism by Paul might be misused to claim a special attachment to him (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: ministers baptize into Christ's name, not their own; the people belong to Christ, not the one who baptizes them.

themes: Lest any should say that I had baptized in mine own name
Paul's concern to give no ground for personal allegiance
baptism into Christ's name
refusal of a following

schools.summary: Paul, recalling more exactly, adds that he also baptized the household of Stephanas (the firstfruits of Achaia, 16:15), and beyond these does not remember baptizing any other; the household baptism often cited in discussions of household baptism, though Paul's point is simply the fewness of those he baptized, his uncertainty showing his honest memory (Barnes, Robertson).

themes: I baptized also the household of Stephanas
I know not whether I baptized any other
Paul's added recollection
baptizing not his chief work

schools.summary: Paul's chief commission was to preach the gospel — not that baptism was forbidden or unimportant, but that preaching was his primary work, baptism often left to others — and to preach 'not with wisdom of words' (rhetorical eloquence, philosophical artifice, the polished oratory the Greeks prized), deliberately renouncing such display (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin (with Henry): had Paul relied on eloquence, the cross would be emptied of its power — men crediting the preacher's skill, not the gospel's truth; the cross's power must shine without the props of human wisdom.

themes: Christ sent me not to baptize, but to preach the gospel
not with wisdom of words, lest the cross of Christ should be made of none effect
the priority of preaching over rhetoric
the cross kept in its power

schools.summary: To those who are perishing (the unbelieving, on the road to ruin), the message of a crucified Saviour appears foolishness (absurd, contemptible); but to those being saved, the same cross is 'the power of God' — the very means by which God exerts his saving, transforming power (Barnes, Robertson).
Chrysostom (with Calvin): the same gospel divides mankind — folly to the perishing, power to the saved; the difference lies not in the message but in the hearers' state.

themes: the preaching of the cross is to them that perish foolishness
unto us which are saved it is the power of God
the cross folly to the perishing, power to the saved
the dividing line of the gospel

schools.summary: Paul quotes Isaiah 29:14 — God will destroy the wisdom of the wise and bring to nothing the understanding of the prudent, the divine purpose to overthrow the proud wisdom of men; applied to the gospel age, God in the cross sets aside the boasted wisdom of philosophers, saving by means they despised (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: it is God's deliberate design to abase human wisdom, that his own wisdom in the cross may shine; the prophecy foretold this overthrow.

themes: I will destroy the wisdom of the wise
bring to nothing the understanding of the prudent
the overthrow of human wisdom
God's design to abase the wise

schools.summary: Paul issues a triumphant challenge: where now are the wise (the Greek philosopher), the scribe (the Jewish teacher), the disputer (the debater, sophist)? — they are confounded, unable to discover or accomplish what the gospel does; for God has shown the world's wisdom to be folly by saving men through the cross which that wisdom could neither devise nor approve (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Wesley): all the schools of human wisdom, Jewish and Greek alike, failed to bring men to God; God has made their vaunted wisdom foolish.

themes: Where is the wise? where is the scribe? where is the disputer of this world
hath not God made foolish the wisdom of this world
the challenge to the sages
the bankruptcy of worldly wisdom

schools.summary: In God's wise arrangement, the world (with all its philosophy) failed by its own wisdom to know God; so God chose to save through 'the foolishness of preaching' (the message men deemed foolish, the cross — not the act of preaching but the thing preached) and to save 'them that believe,' faith the appointed channel (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Wesley): God deliberately bypassed human wisdom, that salvation might be of grace through faith, and the glory wholly his.

themes: in the wisdom of God the world by wisdom knew not God
it pleased God by the foolishness of preaching to save them that believe
God's choice to save by preaching
salvation by grace through faith

schools.summary: The two characteristic demands: the Jews require a sign (a miracle, a display of power, the proof they sought from any claimant), and the Greeks seek after wisdom (philosophical depth, rhetorical and intellectual brilliance); each nation, by its demand, predisposed to reject the cross — the Jews for its weakness, the Greeks for its simplicity (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Wesley): both Jew and Greek brought their own standard to the gospel, and by it stumbled at the cross — each preferring their own demand to God's appointed way.

themes: the Jews require a sign, and the Greeks seek after wisdom
the differing demands of Jews and Greeks
the obstacles to the cross
each stumbling by their own standard

schools.summary: Paul's central proclamation is Christ crucified — not a triumphant sign for the Jews nor a philosophy for the Greeks, but a crucified Messiah, the very thing both rejected; a stumbling-block (skandalon) to the Jews, who expected a conquering deliverer and saw a crucified one as cursed (Barnes, Robertson).
Cyril: to the Greeks a crucified God was absurd, foolishness — beneath the dignity of their philosophy; Paul refused to soften the cross to suit either Jew or Greek.

themes: we preach Christ crucified
unto the Jews a stumblingblock, and unto the Greeks foolishness
Christ crucified the offence of the cross
rejected by Jew and Greek

schools.summary: To those effectually called (by God's grace, whether Jew or Greek), the same crucified Christ appears not as scandal or folly but as the power of God (the means of their salvation, where the Jews sought a sign) and the wisdom of God (God's saving wisdom embodied, where the Greeks sought wisdom), meeting both demands in a higher way (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: it is the calling (God's effectual grace) that opens the eyes to see in the cross what the unbelieving cannot — power and wisdom.

themes: unto them which are called, both Jews and Greeks
Christ the power of God, and the wisdom of God
Christ power and wisdom to the called
the cross transformed by the calling

schools.summary: What men deem foolishness in God (the cross, the gospel scheme) is in truth wiser than the highest human wisdom, and what men deem weakness in God (a crucified, suffering Saviour) is stronger than all human strength, accomplishing what no human power could (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin (with Wesley): God's seeming folly and weakness in the cross infinitely surpass the wisdom and might of men; the contrast is between God at his 'weakest' (so men judge) and man at his strongest.

themes: the foolishness of God is wiser than men
the weakness of God is stronger than men
the paradox of divine folly and weakness
God's surpassing wisdom and power

schools.summary: Paul bids the Corinthians look at their own calling — the kind of people God had called — as evidence: among them were not many wise (the learned), mighty (the powerful), or noble (the high-born); the church was largely composed of the humble and lowly (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Henry, Chrysostom): God deliberately passed by the great of the world, choosing the lowly, that salvation might manifestly be of grace, not of human advantage.

themes: ye see your calling, brethren
not many wise men after the flesh, not many mighty, not many noble, are called
the lowly composition of the called
God's choice of the humble

schools.summary: God deliberately chose (selected) the things the world counts foolish (the lowly, the unlearned) precisely to confound (put to shame, humble) the wise, and the weak (the powerless) to confound the mighty — overthrowing human wisdom and strength by means they despised (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: God's choice of the lowly is deliberate, designed to abase human pride and to display that the power and glory are wholly his.

themes: God hath chosen the foolish things of the world to confound the wise
the weak things of the world to confound the things which are mighty
God confounding the great by the lowly
the deliberate divine strategy

schools.summary: God chose the base (low-born, ignoble) and despised (held in contempt) things of the world — the objects of the world's scorn — and even 'things which are not' (treated as nothing, of no account) to bring to nought (annul) 'things that are' (the esteemed, the established); the lowliest overthrowing the greatest (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: God exalts the nothings and abases the somethings, that all human glory may be levelled and his grace alone exalted.

themes: base things of the world, and things which are despised, hath God chosen
things which are not, to bring to nought things that are
God nullifying the great by the nothing
the radical reversal of the gospel

schools.summary: The grand purpose of God's choosing the lowly: that no flesh (no human being) should glory (boast) in his presence — all ground for human boasting removed; before God no one may claim merit or glory, salvation being wholly of grace (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Wesley): God so orders salvation that all human pride is excluded and the glory is his alone — the great end of the gospel's method.

themes: That no flesh should glory in his presence
the purpose that no flesh should glory
the exclusion of human boasting
all glory to God alone

schools.summary: The believers' whole spiritual being is from God ('of him'), in Christ by grace not merit; Christ is made to us wisdom (true knowledge of God), righteousness, sanctification, and redemption — the whole of salvation in him. But Barnes denies the text proves imputation: it affirms we become righteous through Christ without specifying the mode, just as we become wise through him (not by imputed wisdom); the mode of justification must be learned elsewhere.

Calvin, Gill: Christ made righteousness to us means his obedience is imputed to us for righteousness — we are accepted of God on his account, the righteousness of faith consisting in remission of sins and gracious acceptance; the four benefits (wisdom, righteousness, sanctification, redemption) the entire excellence of Christ, justification and sanctification conjoined yet distinct.

Clarke (with Wesley): righteousness = justification, the remission of sins the law could not give; sanctification a principle of universal holiness wrought by the Spirit; redemption deliverance from the bondage of sin and death — man of himself possessing no good, all coming from God through Christ.

debates.question: Does 'Christ made unto us righteousness' teach the imputation of Christ's righteousness?

sides.position: Yes — Christ's obedience is imputed to us for righteousness, the ground of justification
No — it affirms we become righteous through Christ without specifying the mode (as we become wise through him); imputation must be proved elsewhere

themes: of him are ye in Christ Jesus
who of God is made unto us wisdom, and righteousness, and sanctification, and redemption
Christ made unto us all things
the imputation debate

schools.summary: Paul quotes the sense of Jeremiah 9:24 — he that glories must glory in the Lord, not in himself, his wisdom, or his works, but in God alone; the grand conclusion of the whole passage, since all is of God and in Christ (v.30), the only legitimate boast being in the Lord (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: because God has so arranged salvation that no flesh may glory (v.29) and Christ is made our all (v.30), it follows that our only glorying is in the Lord.

themes: He that glorieth, let him glory in the Lord
the conclusion to glory in the Lord
the believer's only boast
self-glorying excluded
